briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 13, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening Domestic Climate Change Response and Cooperation
sections.content: Domestically, there has been a continuous stream of news regarding various collaborations and technological developments for climate crisis response [1, 2, 4, 60]. The Rights of Nature and the Climate Change Center signed a business agreement for joint response to the climate crisis, while the Incheon Institute and the Korea Climate Change Research Institute also signed an MOU to strengthen climate change response and international cooperation [7, 8]. Notably, KAIST has ushered in a new era of future prediction by developing technology that integrates and forecasts climate, economy, and energy sectors using Artificial Intelligence (AI) [3]. Efforts by local governments and public institutions are also noteworthy: Gwangmyeong City achieved an award for its citizen-participatory carbon neutrality policy [13], and LH introduced smart housing technology combining carbon neutrality and AI [17]. Everyday carbon neutrality practices are also spreading, such as Suwon City's carbon-neutral green city experiment [10], Seowon-gu's promotion of carbon-neutral lifestyle rules [16], and Boseong Yedang Middle School's eco-friendly spring picnic [18].

sections.title: ⚠️ Challenges of Carbon Neutrality Policies
sections.content: Challenges and concerns have also been raised during the transition to carbon neutrality. Voices from civil society repeatedly called for amendments to the Carbon Neutrality Act [11, 14, 19], and the National Human Rights Commission recommended improvements to building energy policies to ensure carbon neutrality policies do not exclude vulnerable social groups [12]. In the industrial sector, it was argued that achieving carbon neutrality in the steel industry would be difficult without power industry reform, suggesting the need for fundamental systemic change [15]. These discussions emphasize the necessity of a more comprehensive and equitable approach to achieve carbon neutrality goals.

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Renewable Energy and Enhanced Economic Viability
sections.content: The economic viability and widespread adoption of renewable energy have once again been confirmed as a global trend. The International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) announced that renewable energy has become cheaper than fossil fuels and has proven its cost competitiveness for stable 24/7 supply [25]. Domestically, Gukmin Solar is pursuing the joint development of 1GW-scale renewable energy as a citizen-led model [20], and Korea Southern Power formed a 'One Team' to expand solar power supply in industrial complexes [21, 26]. Korea Water Resources Corporation (K-water) is accelerating the transition to renewable energy using 'water' and seeking solutions for energy security [23], while Yeonggwang County extended the application period for its integrated renewable energy support project to encourage local-level expansion [24, 28]. Chuncheon's 'Solbau Village' is gaining attention for achieving energy independence through solar power [27], and Representative Park Jie-won emphasized the importance of creating large-scale renewable energy complexes by advocating for the linkage of the Saemangeum RE100 Industrial Complex and southwestern offshore wind power [29]. Internationally, JinkoSolar and Masdar announced the progress of a large-scale project by signing a contract to supply 2GW of solar modules to the UAE [22], and positive perceptions of renewable energy are spreading, with the majority of Utah residents supporting the use of public lands for renewable energy production [46].

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Threats and Policy Reversals
sections.content: Globally, threats from climate change are intensifying, and concerning policy decisions are emerging in some regions. Climate change threatens global mental health and increases PTSD after heatwaves and disasters [6], and warnings have been raised that rainfall could concentrate into larger storms and new types of weather disasters could occur [34, 39]. Impacts across ecosystems have been reported, such as changes in the timing of Colorado River runoff [31] and the endangered status of emperor penguins [50]. Complex issues are also surfacing, including the potential contribution of microplastics to global warming [47] and increased energy demand due to climate change [57]. Economic impacts are also materializing, with the possibility of increased homeowner insurance premiums in Florida due to climate change [36, 41, 48], and environmental justice issues, where climate change disproportionately affects marginalized communities, have been highlighted as a key challenge [53]. Furthermore, the Florida Governor's signing of a bill prohibiting local climate change efforts [43], the Michigan House's vote to repeal clean energy standards [54], Utah's approval of AI data center construction that will cause immense power and water consumption [61], and reports that Western Australia is threatening Australia's climate targets [62] are policy decisions that reverse climate action and raise serious concerns.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights a complex reality: positive progress in domestic and international cooperation for climate crisis response and renewable energy transition, yet simultaneously facing issues of social equity in carbon neutrality policies, global climate threats, and policy reversals in some regions. This is a critical juncture demanding stronger and more comprehensive climate action for a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: ‘Irresponsible’: backlash as Utah approves datacenter twice the size of Manhattan
articles.description: Facility would require more power than entire state uses and suck up vast amount of water in drought-stricken areaA plan to create one of the world’s largest datacenters, a gargantuan project spanning an area more than twice the size of Manhattan, has provoked a furious public backlash in Utah amid concerns over its vast energy use and impact upon the state’s stressed water supplies.The Stratos artificial intelligence datacenter footprint will cover more than 40,000 acres (62 sq miles) over thre

summary.phenomenon: A plan to construct one of the world's largest artificial intelligence data centers, spanning an area more than twice the size of Manhattan (40,000 acres, 62 square miles), has been approved in Utah. This facility is projected to require more power than the entire state uses and consume vast amounts of water in a drought-stricken region, provoking strong public backlash.
summary.cause: The article does not mention the scientific causes or background for the approval of the data center construction plan. However, public opposition stems from concerns over the data center's immense energy consumption and its potential impact on the depleted water resources in the drought-stricken region.
summary.outlook: The article does not mention specific future impacts or outlooks resulting from the data center's construction.

articles.title: Roots of resilience: the experts working to bolster apples against the climate crisis
articles.description: Scientists are focusing on improving apples’ resilience after stressors like wild temperature swings and droughtTerence Robinson still remembers the Valentine’s Day Massacre – of 2015, not 1929.For the Cornell University horticulture professor, the term doesn’t conjure up Tommy guns and Al Capone’s Chicago. Instead of a gangster, the culprit in Robinson’s massacre was the weather. And its victims were the apple orchards of the north-eastern United States. Continue reading...
